app.title: Smanetting
app.subtitle: Interactive photography
app.tagline: Tweak the settings and watch the effect in real time.
switch.approach.label: Rendering
switch.style.label: Style

demos.nav.label: Concepts
compare.hint: A is locked — change the controls to update B
footer.note: Smanetting · an educational project to learn photography
landing.lead: Pick a concept: each one comes with an in-depth explanation and an interactive demo where you move the parameters and see the effect in real time.
landing.anatomy.title: Camera anatomy
landing.anatomy.blurb: The DSLR taken apart in 3D, on scroll, component by component.
anatomy.index.title: Camera anatomy
anatomy.index.lead: Take apart in 3D the three systems that capture the image: the DSLR body, the sensor and the optics. Open an explainer and scroll.
anatomy.reflex.title: DSLR anatomy
anatomy.reflex.blurb: The camera body coming apart: mirror, pentaprism, shutter, sensor.
anatomy.sensor.title: The sensor
anatomy.sensor.blurb: Photosites, microlenses and the Bayer filter: how light becomes an image.
anatomy.optics.title: The optics
anatomy.optics.blurb: Elements, focusing and aperture: how the lens forms the image.
card.shutter.blurb: Freeze the action or turn it into a trail by playing with shutter speed.
card.aperture.blurb: Creamy background or all in focus: aperture and depth of field.
card.iso.blurb: Low light and noise: the sensitivity trade-off.
card.focal.blurb: Angle of view and perspective compression as the focal length changes.
card.triangle.blurb: Shutter, aperture and ISO together: the balance of exposure.
card.histogram.blurb: Read exposure from the graph: no blown highlights or crushed shadows.
card.whiteBalance.blurb: Warm or cool casts: adjust the colour temperature of the light.
card.panning.blurb: Sharp subject, streaked background: follow motion with slow shutter speeds.
card.crop.blurb: The same lens frames differently as the sensor format changes.
card.diffraction.blurb: Stopping down too far softens the image: find the lens sweet spot.
card.portrait.blurb: Big noses and flattened faces: the distance that distorts portraits.
card.hyperfocal.blurb: Everything sharp from foreground to infinity with hyperfocal focusing.
card.metering.blurb: Spot, centre, matrix: how the camera measures the scene's light.
card.stabilization.blurb: Safe shutter and stabilisation: how many stops you gain handheld.
card.flash.blurb: Sync speed and the black band: making flash work with the shutter.
card.bracketing.blurb: Several shots at different exposures merged into HDR to cover the full range.
card.filters.blurb: ND for long exposures, polariser to kill reflections and deepen the sky.
card.modes.blurb: P, A, S, M: what you set and what the camera works out.
card.raw.blurb: RAW or JPEG: how much you can recover shadows and highlights in post.
card.dynamic.blurb: How many stops the sensor captures and what bit depth means.
card.colorSpace.blurb: sRGB, Adobe RGB, ProPhoto: which colours an image can hold.
engine.fallback: {from} isn't ready for this demo yet → shown in the {to} version.
demo.shutter.title: Shutter speed
demo.shutter.lesson.intro: Shutter speed is how long light reaches the sensor: fast speeds freeze motion, slow speeds turn it into a trail.
demo.shutter.lesson.p1: Fast speeds (1/1000 s) → sharp subject, frozen action.
demo.shutter.lesson.p2: Slow speeds (1/8 s) → motion trail and a sense of speed.
demo.shutter.lesson.p3: The trail grows with the subject's speed and with the shutter duration.
demo.aperture.title: Aperture and depth of field
demo.aperture.lesson.intro: Aperture controls how much light enters and how much of the scene stays in focus. Wide apertures (f/1.4) isolate the subject with a blurred background; narrow apertures (f/16) keep everything sharp.
demo.aperture.lesson.p1: Small f = wide aperture → shallow depth of field, nice bokeh.
demo.aperture.lesson.p2: Large f = narrow aperture → extended depth of field.
demo.aperture.lesson.p3: The in-focus band widens by closing the aperture and moving the subject farther away.
demo.iso.title: ISO and noise
demo.iso.lesson.intro: ISO amplifies the sensor signal: it raises brightness in low light, but introduces noise. The higher you go, the more grain grows and quality drops.
demo.iso.lesson.p1: Low ISO (100) → clean image, needs more light.
demo.iso.lesson.p2: High ISO (12800) → visible noise, especially in the shadows.
demo.iso.lesson.p3: Doubling ISO = +1 stop of brightness, but more grain and less dynamic range.
demo.focal.title: Focal length and compression
demo.focal.lesson.intro: Focal length changes the angle of view. With the subject kept the same size (moving closer or farther), a long focal length compresses the background by bringing it closer: 'compression' comes from distance, not from the lens.
demo.focal.lesson.p1: Short focal length (24mm) → wide angle, small and distant background.
demo.focal.lesson.p2: Long focal length (200mm) → narrow angle, large and compressed background.
demo.focal.lesson.p3: The subject stays the same because you change the shooting distance (dolly): that's what compresses.
demo.triangle.title: The exposure triangle
demo.triangle.lesson.intro: Shutter, aperture and ISO together set the exposure. To keep the same brightness you can compensate: open up one stop and shorten the shutter by one stop.
demo.triangle.lesson.p1: Each stop doubles or halves the light: the three parameters balance out.
demo.triangle.lesson.p2: Same exposure, different look: f/2.8 + 1/500 and f/16 + 1/15 give the same light.
demo.triangle.lesson.p3: Meter at 0 = correct; to the right overexposed, to the left underexposed.
demo.hist.title: Histogram and exposure
demo.hist.lesson.intro: The histogram shows how tones are distributed: shadows on the left, highlights on the right. Shifting exposure moves the hump; if it hits the edges, tones are clipped (unrecoverable).
demo.hist.lesson.p1: Hump on the right hitting the edge → blown highlights (in red).
demo.hist.lesson.p2: Hump squashed on the left → blocked shadows (in blue).
demo.hist.lesson.p3: A balanced exposure uses the whole range without needless clipping.
demo.wb.title: White balance
demo.wb.lesson.intro: Every light has its own colour, measured in kelvin: low (2700 K) warm and orange, high (8000 K) cool and blue. White balance tells the camera what the light is like, so whites stay neutral.
demo.wb.lesson.p2: Cool light (shade, overcast sky): blue tint.
demo.wb.lesson.p3: The right temperature neutralises the cast; getting it wrong accentuates it, by mistake or by choice.
demo.panning.title: Panning
demo.panning.lesson.intro: In panning you follow the subject with the camera while shooting at a medium shutter speed: the subject stays sharp and the background streaks, giving a strong sense of speed.
demo.panning.lesson.p1: Too fast a shutter → little streaking, weak effect.
demo.panning.lesson.p2: Medium shutter (1/30 s) → streaked background and sharp subject.
demo.panning.lesson.p3: You need a smooth movement parallel to the subject to keep it in focus.
demo.crop.title: Sensor and crop factor
demo.crop.lesson.intro: With the same lens, a smaller sensor records only the central part of the image: it crops the framing. The crop factor tells you how much, and which equivalent focal length it matches.
demo.crop.lesson.p3: Small sensors = longer telephotos, but less light and less subject separation.
demo.diffraction.title: Diffraction and sweet spot
demo.diffraction.lesson.intro: Lenses aren't sharpest wide open: they're sharpest at a middle point. Wide open, aberrations dominate; stopped down too far, diffraction does.
demo.portrait.title: Portrait distortion
demo.portrait.lesson.intro: Distance from the face changes proportions: up close the nose grows, from afar features look natural.
demo.portrait.lesson.p1: Too close (0.4 m) → enlarged nose, distortion.
demo.portrait.lesson.p3: To fill the frame from afar you need a longer focal length.
demo.hyperfocal.title: Hyperfocal and zone focusing
demo.hyperfocal.lesson.intro: The hyperfocal distance maximises depth of field: in focus from half of it to infinity.
demo.hyperfocal.lesson.p1: Closing the aperture brings the hyperfocal closer and widens the sharp zone.
demo.hyperfocal.lesson.p2: Focused on the hyperfocal → sharp all the way to infinity.
demo.hyperfocal.lesson.p3: Street technique: f/8, focus on the hyperfocal, always ready.
demo.metering.title: Metering modes
demo.metering.lesson.intro: The meter measures light and aims for middle grey. The modes decide which part of the scene to weight: spot, centre or the whole frame (matrix).
demo.metering.lesson.p1: Matrix → averages the whole scene, balanced.
demo.metering.lesson.p2: Spot → reads only the subject, ideal in backlight.
demo.metering.lesson.p3: It aims for middle grey: snow and black fool it, compensate.
demo.stabilization.title: Stabilisation and safe shutter
demo.stabilization.lesson.intro: Handheld, shake causes blur if the shutter exceeds 1/(focal). Stabilisation gains stops, but doesn't freeze a moving subject.
demo.stabilization.lesson.p3: IS won't stop the subject: for that you need a fast shutter.
demo.flash.title: Flash and sync speed
demo.flash.lesson.intro: The shutter exposes the whole sensor only up to the sync speed. Faster, a slit travels across the sensor and the flash leaves a black band.
demo.flash.lesson.p1: Within sync (1/250 s) → whole frame lit.
demo.flash.lesson.p3: To go faster you need High-Speed Sync (HSS).
demo.bracketing.title: Bracketing / HDR
demo.bracketing.lesson.intro: One exposure captures a limited dynamic range. Bracketing: several shots at different exposures, merged into HDR, cover highlights and shadows.
demo.bracketing.lesson.p1: Single shot → clipping of highlights and shadows.
demo.bracketing.lesson.p2: More spacing between shots → more range covered.
demo.bracketing.lesson.p3: Needs a still subject or tripod to merge without artefacts.
demo.filters.title: Filters: ND and polariser
demo.filters.lesson.intro: Filters act in front of the lens. An ND cuts light neutrally so you can use longer shutter speeds; a polariser (CPL) removes reflections and kills glare.
demo.filters.lesson.p1: ND → less light at the same exposure → long shutter (silky water and clouds).
demo.filters.lesson.p2: Polariser → removes reflections on water and glass, saturates the sky, reveals what's underwater.
demo.filters.lesson.p3: The CPL effect peaks at 90° from the sun and is set by rotating the filter.
demo.modes.title: P / A / S / M modes
demo.modes.lesson.intro: Modes decide who chooses what. In A you set the aperture and the camera works out the shutter; in S it's the opposite; in M you set both; in P the camera sets both.
demo.modes.lesson.p1: A (aperture priority): you control depth of field, the camera keeps the exposure.
demo.modes.lesson.p2: S (shutter priority): you control motion blur, the camera adjusts the aperture.
demo.modes.lesson.p3: M: full control, but the exposure can be off if you ignore the meter.
scene.modes.desc.P: the camera picks shutter and aperture
scene.modes.desc.A: you set aperture, camera sets shutter
scene.modes.desc.S: you set shutter, camera sets aperture
demo.raw.title: RAW vs JPEG
demo.raw.lesson.intro: RAW keeps all the sensor data (12–14 bit); JPEG is already developed and compressed to 8 bit. The difference shows when you push shadows and highlights in post.
demo.raw.lesson.p1: RAW → wide latitude: you recover shadows and highlights cleanly.
demo.raw.lesson.p2: JPEG → push too far and banding, colour noise and blown highlights appear.
demo.raw.lesson.p3: Shoot RAW if you plan to edit; JPEG is fine when the exposure is already right.
demo.dynamic.title: Dynamic range and bit depth
demo.dynamic.lesson.intro: Dynamic range is the number of stops between the darkest shadows and the brightest highlights the sensor records. Beyond that limit detail is lost; bits split that range into tonal levels.
demo.dynamic.lesson.p1: Scene beyond the sensor's range → blown highlights and/or crushed shadows.
demo.dynamic.lesson.p2: More bits → more tonal levels → less banding in gradients after editing.
demo.dynamic.lesson.p3: For very contrasty scenes: expose to the right, shoot RAW or bracket.
demo.colorSpace.title: Colour spaces
fig.cap.motion: Fast shutter: the subject moves a few pixels and stays sharp. Slow shutter: it records the trail.
fig.cap.aperture: The f-number is focal length over pupil diameter; the blade count shapes the bokeh highlights.
fig.cap.iso: Visible noise tracks the photons gathered (√light): it's the lack of light, more than the ISO.
fig.cap.triangle: Aperture, shutter and ISO add up in stops: fix the exposure and moving one forces a trade in another.
fig.cap.modes: Who sets what: in A you pick the aperture, in S the shutter, in M both, in P neither.
fig.cap.metering: The scene area the meter samples: a spot, the weighted centre, or the whole frame in zones.
fig.cap.stab: Stabilisation corrects hand shake on 5 axes; it cannot freeze a moving subject, though.
fig.cap.aov: Angle of view depends on focal length and sensor size: AoV = 2·arctan(d/2f).
fig.cap.compression: 'Compression' is distance, not focal length: up close the background is small, far away it looms.
fig.cap.crop: A smaller sensor crops the centre of the image: same focal length, tighter framing.
fig.cap.diffraction: Falling aberrations and rising diffraction cross: that crossing is the sharpness sweet spot.
fig.cap.portrait: Up close the nose enlarges; stepping back (longer lens) normalises the proportions.
fig.cap.hyperfocal: Focusing at the hyperfocal H, everything from H/2 to infinity is acceptably sharp.
fig.cap.kelvin: Colour temperature in Kelvin: white balance applies the opposite correction to the light.
fig.cap.histogram: Pixel count from shadows to highlights; against a wall = clipping (lost detail).
fig.cap.panning: Following the subject at a slow shutter keeps it sharp while the background streaks.
fig.cap.flash: Beyond the sync speed the shutter is a slit: the flash leaves a black band. HSS pulses.
fig.cap.polarizer: A polariser works best 90° from the sun: there it deepens the sky and cuts reflections.
fig.cap.raw: RAW is a Bayer mosaic (one colour per pixel): demosaicing reconstructs the three channels.
fig.cap.dynamic: Dynamic range is the usable band between highlight clipping and the noise floor, in stops.
fig.cap.bracketing: Several shots at different exposures, merged, cover a wider range than a single frame.
fig.cap.colorspace: The CIE horseshoe holds all visible colours; each space is a triangle between its primaries.
fig.cap.thirds: The 3×3 grid: subject on a line or intersection, horizon on a third rather than the middle.
fig.cap.golden: The phi grid divides at 0.382 and 0.618; the golden spiral grows by φ every quarter-turn.
fig.cap.leading: Leading lines carry the eye to the subject; leave it lead room in front.
card.composition.blurb: Rule of thirds, golden ratio and leading lines: how to arrange elements in the frame.
demo.composition.title: Framing & composition
demo.composition.ctrl.subject: Subject (horiz.)
demo.composition.lesson.intro: Composition guides the eye. Move the subject and horizon and try the grids: when the subject lands on a power point, the image "settles".
demo.composition.lesson.p1: Subject on a line or intersection → more dynamic than dead-centre.
demo.composition.lesson.p2: Horizon on a third: low for the sky, high for the foreground.
demo.composition.lesson.p3: Grids are guides, not laws: the centre works for symmetry and reflections.
